narration.text: What happens when a team builds an entire product with zero manually-written code? Not a weekend prototype. A real internal beta, roughly one million lines of code, shipped to hundreds of users over five months. This is the story of harness engineering.

The team at OpenAI started from an empty git repository in late August 2025. Three engineers, later growing to seven, opened and merged around 1,500 pull requests. Average throughput: three and a half PRs per engineer per day. Estimated ten times faster than hand-writing. And the core rule: humans never directly wrote any code.

So what is harness engineering? It's the discipline of designing environments, scaffolding, feedback loops, and control systems that enable AI coding agents to produce and maintain reliable software at scale. The core shift: humans steer, agents execute. The engineer's job is no longer writing code, but building the harness, the tooling, abstractions, documentation, and review systems that allow agents to do useful work autonomously.
When something fails, the fix is never try harder. It's always: what capability is missing, and how do we make it both legible and enforceable for the agent? This is the fundamental mindset shift.

The first key concept is agent legibility. The bottleneck shifted from code throughput to human QA capacity. The solution: make the application itself directly legible to the agent. The app is bootable per git worktree. Chrome DevTools Protocol is wired into the agent runtime for DOM snapshots, screenshots, and navigation. The observability stack, logs, metrics, and traces, is exposed per worktree.

This means agents can reproduce bugs, validate fixes, and reason about UI behavior. Prompts like ensure service startup completes in under 800 milliseconds become tractable. Single agent runs regularly work for six or more hours, often while humans sleep.

The second key concept is repository knowledge as the system of record. The team learned a critical lesson: give the agent a map, not a thousand-page instruction manual. The one big AGENTS.md approach failed because context is scarce, and a giant file crowds out the actual task. When everything is important, nothing is. Monolithic manuals rot instantly.

Instead, AGENTS.md is a roughly 100-line table of contents pointing to a structured docs directory. Design docs, execution plans, product specs, references. Progressive disclosure means agents start with a small, stable entry point and are taught where to look next. Mechanically enforced: linters and CI validate the knowledge base is up-to-date and cross-linked.

The repository is optimized for the agent's legibility first, not humans'. Anything the agent can't access in context effectively doesn't exist. Knowledge in Google Docs, Slack, or people's heads is illegible to the system. The team pushes more and more context into the repo over time.

Now let's talk about enforcing architecture and taste. The philosophy is: enforce invariants, not micromanage implementations. The team uses a rigid architectural model where each business domain is divided into fixed layers with strictly validated dependency directions.

The layer order is: Types, then Config, then Repo, then Service, then Runtime, then UI. Cross-cutting concerns like auth, connectors, and telemetry enter through a single explicit interface called Providers. This is enforced mechanically via custom linters and structural tests, all generated by the agent itself.

A brilliant technique: custom lint error messages are written to inject remediation instructions into agent context. When the linter fires, the error message tells the agent exactly how to fix the problem. This means documentation rules get promoted into code when docs alone don't hold.

Another key insight: favor boring technology. Composable, stable APIs that are well-represented in training data. Sometimes it's cheaper to reimplement functionality than to work around opaque upstream libraries. The team built their own concurrency helper instead of using a generic package, tightly integrated with their telemetry, with 100 percent test coverage.

The merge philosophy is also different at high throughput. Minimal blocking merge gates. PRs are short-lived. Test flakes are addressed with follow-up runs rather than blocking indefinitely. The mantra: corrections are cheap, and waiting is expensive. Though they acknowledge this would be irresponsible in low-throughput environments.

One of the most interesting challenges is entropy. The agent replicates existing patterns, including suboptimal ones, leading to drift. Initially, the team spent every Friday, twenty percent of the week, cleaning up what they called AI slop. That didn't scale.

The solution: golden principles encoded into the repository, plus a recurring cleanup process. Example golden principles: prefer shared utility packages over hand-rolled helpers, and don't probe data recklessly, validate boundaries or rely on typed SDKs.

Background agent tasks run on a regular cadence: scanning for deviations, updating quality grades, opening targeted refactoring PRs. Most are reviewable in under a minute and can be auto-merged. This functions like garbage collection. Technical debt is like a high-interest loan, almost always better to pay it down continuously in small increments than to let it compound.

At the highest level of autonomy, a single prompt can drive an entire feature. The agent validates the current codebase state, reproduces a reported bug, records a video demonstrating the failure, implements a fix, validates the fix by driving the application, records a second video showing the resolution, opens a pull request, responds to feedback, detects build failures, and escalates to a human only when judgment is required.

The core takeaway from harness engineering: building software still demands discipline, but the discipline shows up more in the scaffolding rather than the code. The engineer's value shifts from writing correct code to designing systems that make correct code the path of least resistance for agents.

q-mindset.title: The Harness Mindset
q-mindset.desc: When an AI agent fails at a task, what is the harness engineering response?
options: Rewrite the prompt with more detail
Identify the missing capability and make it legible and enforceable
Switch to a more capable model

correct.title: Exactly!
correct.body: The fix is never 'try harder.' It's always: what capability is missing, and how do we make it both legible and enforceable for the agent?

wrong.title: Not quite
wrong.body: Better prompts or models are temporary fixes. Harness engineering asks: what structural capability is missing from the environment?

try-sort-repo.title: Order the Knowledge Layers
try-sort-repo.desc: Drag to arrange from entry point to deepest detail.

correct.title: Perfect!
correct.body: Progressive disclosure: the agent starts with a short map, then navigates to the level of detail it needs. Never a monolithic manual.

wrong.title: Check the order
wrong.body: AGENTS.md is the entry point. Architecture gives the overview. Domain docs provide area-specific detail. Design docs and plans go deepest.

q-legibility.title: Agent Legibility
q-legibility.desc: Why did the 'one big AGENTS.md' approach fail?
options: The file was too large for the model's context window
Context is scarce, and a giant file crowds out the actual task
The formatting was hard for the model to parse

correct.title: Right!
correct.body: When everything is 'important,' nothing is. A monolithic file consumes precious context that should be used for the task itself.

wrong.title: Not quite
wrong.body: The issue isn't technical limits or formatting. It's that a giant file wastes context that the agent needs for the actual task.

try-categorize-enforcement.title: Enforcement Mechanisms
try-categorize-enforcement.desc: Drag each technique into the right enforcement category.

correct.title: Nice!
correct.body: The key insight: when documentation falls short, promote the rule into code. Automated enforcement is more reliable than written guidelines.

wrong.title: Close
wrong.body: Linters, tests, and CI checks are automated enforcement in code. Architecture docs, design docs, and golden principles are documentation-based.

q-merge.title: Merge Philosophy
q-merge.desc: What is the merge mantra in high-throughput harness engineering?
options: Corrections are cheap, and waiting is expensive
Every PR needs two human approvals

correct.title: Exactly!
correct.body: At high throughput, minimal blocking merge gates make sense. But they acknowledge this would be irresponsible in low-throughput environments.

wrong.title: Not quite
wrong.body: It's not reckless speed or heavy process. The key insight is that at high throughput, the cost of waiting exceeds the cost of correcting.

try-explore-entropy.title: Fighting Entropy
try-explore-entropy.desc: Tap each strategy to learn how it combats code drift.

hotspots.label: Golden Principles
hotspots.explanation: Encode core standards like 'prefer shared utilities over hand-rolled helpers' into the repository. Agents follow what they can see.

hotspots.label: Recurring Cleanup Agents
hotspots.explanation: Background tasks scan for deviations, update quality grades, and open targeted refactoring PRs on a regular cadence.

hotspots.label: Quality Grades
hotspots.explanation: Automated scoring of code areas. PRs that degrade a grade get flagged. Most cleanup PRs are reviewable in under a minute.

hotspots.label: Boring Technology
hotspots.explanation: Composable, stable APIs well-represented in training data. Sometimes cheaper to reimplement than to fight opaque dependencies.

correct.body: Technical debt is like a high-interest loan. Better to pay it down continuously in small increments than let it compound.

wrong.body: Explore all four strategies to continue.

q-autonomy.title: Levels of Autonomy
q-autonomy.desc: At the highest autonomy level, when does the agent escalate to a human?
options: After every pull request for review
When it encounters any test failure

correct.title: Right!
correct.body: The agent handles the full loop: reproduce, fix, validate, PR, respond to feedback, detect build failures. It escalates only when human judgment is needed.

wrong.title: Not quite
wrong.body: The agent can handle reviews, test failures, and build issues on its own. It only escalates when genuine human judgment is required.
